title: Reading and Use of English - Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about four people who share how their cultural background or traditions influence their daily life. For questions 43–52, choose from the people (A–D). The people may be chosen more than once.

content.title: Roots and Rhythms: Four People on Cultural Traditions and Heritage

texts.A:
### Mei, 28, Fashion Designer
[43]Growing up in a Chinese household in London, I spent my childhood moving between two worlds that felt almost entirely separate.[/43] At school I was British; at home, Chinese – and for many years I experienced that as a contradiction rather than a richness. [47]It was only when I began incorporating traditional Chinese textile patterns and embroidery techniques into my fashion designs that the two worlds started to feel like a single, integrated identity.[/47] [51]My collection last year drew entirely on the visual language of my grandmother's province, and the response from the Chinese diaspora community was more moving than any critical review could ever be.[/51]
texts.B:
### Ibrahim, 45, Restaurant Owner
[44]Food is how my family has always maintained its connection to Morocco, despite having lived in Manchester for two generations.[/44] The recipes my mother taught me are not written down anywhere – they exist in the memory of my hands. [48]Running the restaurant means I am effectively a cultural ambassador every time someone sits down at one of my tables, which is a responsibility I take seriously and find deeply fulfilling.[/48] [52]I worry sometimes that my own children, who have grown up more British than Moroccan, will lose the thread of these traditions when I am no longer here to pass them on.[/52] That fear is part of why I recently started running cooking workshops for the local Moroccan community.
texts.C:
### Katarzyna, 33, Librarian
[45]I came to England from Poland at nineteen and made what I now think was a mistake – I suppressed my Polish identity for years in a effort to integrate fully.[/45] I stopped speaking Polish at home, stopped celebrating Polish traditions, and gradually lost fluency in my mother tongue. [49]In my late twenties I experienced a profound sense of loss and began deliberately reclaiming the culture I had set aside – learning Polish again, reconnecting with Polish literature, and celebrating traditions like Wigilia for the first time in years.[/49] The process has been both painful and joyful, and it has made me passionate about the importance of cultural preservation.
texts.D:
### Marcus, 60, Retired Teacher
[46]I was born in Jamaica and came to England as a child, and the experience of navigating two cultures simultaneously shaped everything about who I became.[/46] My parents were determined to maintain Jamaican traditions in our home – the food, the music, the storytelling – while also equipping us to succeed in British society. [50]I have thought deeply about what my parents gave me, and I believe the greatest gift was not any specific tradition but the understanding that identity is something you build rather than simply inherit.[/50] In retirement I run a community history project documenting the experiences of the Windrush generation, because I believe those stories deserve to be preserved and told properly.

questions.question: describes experiencing their dual cultural identity as a source of conflict during their upbringing?
questions.explanation: Mei spent her childhood moving between two worlds that felt almost entirely separate, experiencing the duality as a contradiction rather than a richness.

questions.question: describes a particular tradition through which their family maintains its connection to their country of origin?
questions.explanation: Ibrahim says food is how his family has always maintained its connection to Morocco, despite two generations in Manchester.

questions.question: made a conscious decision early in life to distance themselves from their cultural roots?
questions.explanation: Katarzyna suppressed her Polish identity for years after arriving in England, stopping speaking Polish at home and abandoning traditions in order to integrate.

questions.question: describes parents who actively worked to balance cultural heritage with adaptation to their new country?
questions.explanation: Marcus says his parents maintained Jamaican traditions at home while also equipping their children to succeed in British society.

questions.question: found that their professional work became the means through which they reconciled their two cultural identities?
questions.explanation: Mei began incorporating Chinese textile patterns and embroidery into her fashion designs, and the two worlds started to feel like a single integrated identity.

questions.question: sees their work as involving a responsibility to represent their culture to others?
questions.explanation: Ibrahim describes himself as a cultural ambassador every time someone sits at his tables, a responsibility he takes seriously.

questions.question: describes actively working to recover cultural knowledge and practices they had previously given up?
questions.explanation: Katarzyna began deliberately reclaiming her Polish culture in her late twenties – relearning the language, reconnecting with literature, and celebrating traditions again.

questions.question: shares a broader reflection about what cultural identity fundamentally means?
questions.explanation: Marcus says the greatest gift from his parents was the understanding that identity is something you build rather than simply inherit.

questions.question: mentions receiving an emotionally significant response from a particular community to their work?
questions.explanation: Mei's collection drew on the visual language of her grandmother's province, and the response from the Chinese diaspora community was more moving than any critical review.

questions.question: expresses concern about whether their cultural traditions will be continued by the next generation?
questions.explanation: Ibrahim worries that his children, who have grown up more British than Moroccan, will lose the thread of these traditions when he is no longer there to pass them on.
